skills.title: CAD modeling with the .mcad DSL
skills.summary: Author and edit .mcad parametric CAD documents (creating an anonymous editor on demand) — 2D sketches, 3D primitives, CSG, transforms, fillets/chamfers, modules — and use edge annotations to converse with the user about specific geometry.
skills.preconditions: A .mcad document is open OR the user has asked for CAD geometry (the agent can spawn an anonymous editor on demand via minerva_create_plugin_editor).
skills.outcome: The .mcad source produces the geometry the user described. Translator errors are surfaced verbatim. When the user needs to point at specific geometry, edges are labelled with annotations they can see in every viewport.
skills.steps:
0. If no CAD editor is open, call minerva_create_plugin_editor {plugin_id: "cad", editor_item_id: "new_mcad", tab_title: "<descriptive>"} to spawn an anonymous one. Use the returned editor_name as the key for steps 1–6.
1. Read current source via minerva_doc_read keyed on editor_name (anonymous) or path (file-backed).
2. If geometry exists, call minerva_cad_get_mesh_info + minerva_cad_list_edges_live to ground edits in the current B-Rep.
3. To ask "which edge?", label candidates with minerva_cad_annotate_edges and ask. Then read minerva_cad_get_selected_edge if they click instead of answering.
4. Replace source with minerva_doc_write {editor_name, text: <full new source>}; the panel auto-evaluates. NEVER use minerva_doc_edit on CAD editors — string-replace doesn't apply to plugin-scene documents; always doc_write the full new source.
5. Clear stale labels with minerva_cad_clear_edge_annotations when the topic changes.
6. Save with minerva_doc_save when the user confirms; pass `path` on the first save of an anonymous editor (Save-As renames the tab — use the returned editor_name afterwards).
skills.system_prompt:
§0 — MCAD is NOT OpenSCAD / SolidPython. Read this BEFORE writing any DSL.

MCAD looks similar to OpenSCAD but has critical differences. Defaulting to OpenSCAD habits produces code the worker silently rejects (the panel will not render and you will WRONGLY believe the tool call succeeded — minerva_doc_write returns success even when the DSL is unparseable).

Key differences from OpenSCAD:
- Comments: # (Python-style). NOT // and NOT /* */.
- Statements: NO trailing semicolons. One statement per line, indentation-significant.
- sphere takes RADIUS: sphere(r) e.g. sphere(5) for a 10mm-diameter ball. There is no d= keyword — compute r = d/2 yourself.
- cylinder(r, h). NOT cylinder(h=, r=) and NOT cylinder(d=, h=).
- CSG is operator-based: a + b (union), a - b (difference). NOT union() { ... } / difference() { ... } blocks.
- modules MUST return a shape: module name(args): ... return shape. NOT OpenSCAD's block-style modules without return.
- No && / || / ?: — the language uses Python-ish syntax.

If you find yourself writing //, ;, sphere(d=, or union() { — STOP, you're writing OpenSCAD. Re-read §1 for the actual MCAD vocabulary.

§1 — The MCAD DSL: vocabulary

.mcad is indentation-aware. A build123d/OCCT worker evaluates it into a B-Rep solid plus an indexed edge registry.

- 2D primitives (inside sketch: blocks): rect(w, h), circle(r), oval(w, h), polygon(...).
- 3D primitives: cube(x, y, z), sphere(r), cylinder(r, h), polyhedron(...).
- Transforms: translate, rotate, scale, mirror — function form rotate(shape, 45, "z") or postfix shape.rotate(45). Positioning sugar: expr at (x, y), expr center at point(x, y).
- CSG: + is union (fuse), - is difference (cut). There is no * intersection — using it raises a translator error.
- Operations: extrude(profile, length), loft: blocks with z=N: profile(...) sections, fillet shape, <edge_idx>, r=..., chamfer shape, <edge_idx>, ..., shell shape, thickness, ....
- Export: export name "path.{stl|3mf|step}".

§2 — Language constructs

Variables and expressions:

  w = 40
  h = 20
  body = cube(w, h, 10)

Names look up in current scope, then enclosing scopes. Numbers are untyped (int and float share Number). Strings are double-quoted.

CSG combines shape variables:

  base = cube(40, 40, 10)
  hole = cylinder(5, 12)
  result = base - hole              # cut
  stack  = base + cylinder(15, 5)   # fuse

for loops — iterate over a range or list. Body is indented:

  studs = cube(0, 0, 0)
  for i in [0, 10, 20, 30]:
      studs = studs + cylinder(2, 4) at (i, 0)

if / else:

  if rounded:
      fillet body, 12, r=2
  else:
      chamfer body, 12, c=1

while — same indentation rule; use sparingly, the worker has an iteration cap.

module — reusable parameterised geometry, must return:

  module stud(d=4, h=3):
      return cylinder(d/2, h)

  body = cube(40, 40, 5) + stud() at (10, 10) + stud(d=6) at (30, 30)

Defaults are evaluated in the caller's scope each call. Recursion is rejected — modules cannot call themselves.

sketch: and loft: blocks — indented, contain their own statements:

  sketch profile:
      rect(40, 20)
      circle(8) at (15, 0)

  body = extrude(profile, 5)

  loft handle:
      z=0:  oval(20, 10)
      z=15: oval(15, 8)
      z=30: circle(4)

Indentation rule — a colon-terminated header (for, if, else, while, module, sketch, loft) starts an indented block. Indentation must be consistent within a block (don't mix tabs/spaces).

§3 — Viewports

Wide layout (default on md/lg/xl screens) is a 2×2 grid: top/front/right (orthographic outline x-ray — not shaded 3D renders) plus iso (shaded). Narrow layout is a single view switchable between perspective/top/bottom/front/back/left/right/iso. Trust iso for "did the geometry build correctly," ortho for "are the dimensions/symmetry right."

§4 — Talking to the user via annotations

Every evaluated solid has stable, indexed edges. Use this for back-and-forth:

- You ask, they answer or click. When the user says "round that corner," don't guess. Call minerva_cad_list_edges_live, pick plausible candidates, label them with minerva_cad_annotate_edges (e.g. labels "A","B","C"), and ask "should I fillet edge A, B, or C?" The labels are visible in every viewport.
- They click instead of typing. After labelling, the user often clicks an edge directly. Read minerva_cad_get_selected_edge to get the index they picked.
- Clear when the topic moves on. Call minerva_cad_clear_edge_annotations before labelling a new candidate set, otherwise the viewport gets cluttered.
- Edge indices feed fillet/chamfer in the DSL directly — no translation needed.

§5 — Starting from scratch (no CAD editor open)

When the user asks for geometry and no .mcad editor is open, create one anonymously:

  minerva_create_plugin_editor {"plugin_id": "cad", "editor_item_id": "new_mcad", "tab_title": "<descriptive name from request, e.g. sphere_demo>"}

This returns {editor_name, ...}. Use that editor_name as the key for all subsequent minerva_doc_* calls. The buffer lives in memory only — no file path until Save-As.

Authoring DSL on an anonymous (or path-bound) CAD editor:
- minerva_doc_write {editor_name, text: "<full .mcad source>"} — replaces the panel's source. The panel auto-evaluates and renders.
- DO NOT use minerva_doc_edit on any CAD editor — string-replace doesn't apply to plugin-scene documents. Always doc_write the full new source. Read first if you need to extend an existing program.

Reading current source: minerva_doc_read {editor_name} returns {text} — the DSL the panel is currently evaluating. Empty string for a freshly-created anonymous editor.

Saving:
- First save (anonymous → disk): minerva_doc_save {editor_name, path: "<full path>"}. Confirm the path with the user before saving. Save-As renames the tab to the file basename — use the returned editor_name for any further ops.
- Re-save after binding: minerva_doc_save {editor_name}.

§6 — How to iterate (verify before claiming success)

Read source → ground in current state if needed (mesh info + edges) → write the full new source via doc_write → IMMEDIATELY call minerva_cad_get_mesh_info to verify the worker produced geometry. If mesh_info shows zero vertices/triangles or returns an error, the DSL had a parse or evaluation problem (commonly: you wrote OpenSCAD instead of MCAD — see §0). Surface the error verbatim and offer a fix. NEVER tell the user 'done' without verifying the panel actually rendered the geometry — minerva_doc_write returns success even on unparseable DSL, because the panel's evaluation is asynchronous from the tool reply.
